content.text: What Are MCP Roots?

MCP roots are a set of URIs that the client sends to the server at initialization time to define the scope of the workspace the server is allowed to operate within. They are analogous to the working directories or open folders the user has granted to the tool — the server is expected to confine its file access, search, and resource exposure to those paths.

Roots are a client-to-server signal, not an enforced access control layer in the protocol. The server receives the list and is expected to honour it. A well-behaved server will refuse to read, write, or expose resources outside these roots; an adversarial or buggy server is not physically prevented by MCP itself.
Purpose:
tell the server which parts of the user's filesystem (or other resource namespace) are in scope.
Direction:
client → server, declared in the initialization handshake and updatable at runtime.
Format:
array of URI objects; currently only file:// URIs are in common use, but the spec allows any URI scheme.

attrs.alt: MCP Roots: client scopes server access to specific workspace paths

content.text: The Roots Protocol
During initialization, a client that supports roots advertises the capability in its ClientCapabilities. The server then calls roots/list to fetch the current set:
When the user opens or closes a folder in the host application, the client sends a notification to the server:
Reading Roots in the Python SDK
The Python MCP SDK provides access to roots through the request context. A server retrieves them by calling list_roots() on the session:
Enforcing Roots in File Operations

A server that accesses the filesystem must validate that every path it reads or writes falls under one of the declared roots. The safe_path() helper pattern used for path traversal defence applies here too:
Handling Root Change Notifications
When roots change mid-session (user opens a new folder, closes a project), the server receives a notifications/roots/list_changed notification. The server should refresh its cached root list:

from mcp.server import Server
from mcp import types

app = Server("live-workspace-tool")

# Cache roots to avoid calling list_roots() on every tool invocation
_cached_roots: list[types.Root] = []

@app.list_roots()  # This decorator handles the change notification
async def on_roots_changed():
    """Called automatically when the client sends notifications/roots/list_changed."""
    ctx = app.request_context
    result = await ctx.session.list_roots()
    global _cached_roots
    _cached_roots = result.roots
    # Optionally: invalidate caches, re-index files, re-expose resources
    return types.EmptyResult()

@app.call_tool()
async def call_tool(name: str, arguments: dict):
    if name == "list_roots":
        root_list = [
            f"{r.name}: {r.uri}" for r in _cached_roots
        ]
        return [types.TextContent(
            type="text",
            text="\n".join(root_list) if root_list else "No roots declared."
        )]

    raise ValueError(f"Unknown tool: {name}")
Checking Roots Capability
Not all clients declare roots. A server should gracefully degrade when roots are absent rather than failing the tool call:
Exposing Resources Scoped to Roots

Servers that expose resources (files, documents, database tables) should restrict the resource list to items reachable through the declared roots. This prevents the server from leaking information about paths outside the user's workspace:
Roots vs Path Traversal Defence
Roots and path traversal prevention address related but distinct concerns:
Concern
Roots
Path Traversal Defence
What it limits
Which top-level directories are in scope
Whether a path escapes its intended directory
Who declares it
The client (host)
The server (hardcoded policy)
Enforcement
Server self-enforces by convention
Server enforces via Path.relative_to() checks
Attack it prevents
Scope creep — tools wandering the filesystem
Injection via ../../../etc/passwd in arguments
When to use
Every file-system server
Every server accepting path arguments
Use both. Roots define where the server is allowed to work; path traversal defence ensures no argument can escape that location.
Testing Roots Handling

import pytest
import tempfile
from pathlib import Path
from unittest.mock import AsyncMock, MagicMock
from mcp import types

async def test_find_files_within_root(tmp_path):
    # Create some test files
    (tmp_path / "main.py").write_text("print('hello')")
    (tmp_path / "utils.py").write_text("# utils")
    (tmp_path / "README.md").write_text("# Readme")

    # Mock session returning tmp_path as the only root
    mock_session = MagicMock()
    mock_session.list_roots = AsyncMock(return_value=MagicMock(
        roots=[MagicMock(uri=f"file://{tmp_path}", name="test")]
    ))

    mock_ctx = MagicMock()
    mock_ctx.session = mock_session

    with patch.object(app, "request_context", mock_ctx):
        result = await call_tool("find_files", {"pattern": "**/*.py"})

    text = result[0].text
    assert "main.py" in text
    assert "utils.py" in text
    assert "README.md" not in text

async def test_resolve_outside_root_rejected(tmp_path):
    mock_session = MagicMock()
    mock_session.list_roots = AsyncMock(return_value=MagicMock(
        roots=[MagicMock(uri=f"file://{tmp_path}", name="test")]
    ))

    with pytest.raises(PermissionError, match="outside all declared roots"):
        await resolve_within_roots("/etc/passwd", mock_session)
Summary

MCP roots give clients a way to declare which filesystem paths (or resource URIs) are in scope for the current session. Servers read the list via roots/list at startup and re-read it when they receive a notifications/roots/list_changed event. A well-behaved server restricts all file reads, writes, and resource listings to paths under the declared roots, combining roots enforcement with path traversal checks to create a layered containment boundary. When roots are absent, servers degrade gracefully rather than operating without scope constraints.
